Okay, so super, super arguably the most important point that I'll make and I probably say that a lot, but they're all important points. That is if you have a small owner operated business, especially like a gym facility, right? Your competitive advantage has to be customer service and a superior customer experience. And I want to underline the fact that that does not mean that your workouts are hard. So for example, you know, I've had a million trainers and you know, I had, you know, one of my trainers was like a 19 year old kid and he, uh, he would just like, he'd have people just do burpees to death. You know what I mean? And it got to the point where I'm like, dude, like, stop, like change it up, bring some variety. You know what I mean? And he was like, dude, they always die when I do that. And the thing is, is that like a 19 year old can tell someone to do 500 burpees and it can be really, really hard and everyone can feel worked, but that is not a superior customer experience. And the service level is low. Okay. And, and I really want to underline this because like that is the competitive advantage is having a superior experience for your customers and having amazing service, having competent employees who know how to treat customers and how to deal with questions appropriately and concerns, etc. Okay. And just having and going over and above to try and wow them at every step, even at, I like to say is like just because like, we just want to send you a card or a gift just because we love you. That's it. No special occasion just because you can continue to over deliver. And what that allows you is it allows you to charge a premium for your service and it gives you forgiveness, which is also really, really important. If someone like, you know, if Amazon messed up my order once, I probably wouldn't quit Prime. I'd be like, I love Prime. You know what I mean? I get my stuff immediately. It's great. And, and that's because I had given them forgiveness because they've done so many good things to me in the past and they've over delivered and they say it was going to be two days and it was one day so many times. Then I'm like, well, don't worry about it guys. Like it's fine. Like I owe you one anyways because you're amazing, right? And it's the same thing and those and that experience is how people start referring their friends because they're proud. They want their friends to experience the same thing they're experiencing. And I'm going to go out on a limb and say, and this is going to be controversial, but I'm going to say it anyways. No one cares about how hard you work out is one. And the second one is results don't matter as much for retention. Okay. Now this is going to sound crazy. Okay. It's going to sound crazy. And my proof in the pudding is that how many gyms do I know who have tons of before and after pictures and are still struggling? Tons. We didn't generate more referrals because of all of the results we were creating. You generate referrals because of the experience that people are getting. Now in the business space, it's slightly different because like when people make tons of money and blah blah blah, it's like they're going to tell their friends who need to make it too. But like within the fitness space, this is my experience. If you're like, we just we get the best results out of anyone. Like if you have really great results, but your customer service sucks, people are going to refer. And if your customer experience is below average and your gym is dirty all the time and your trainers show up late. But you know, people get results. It's not going to create referrals and it's not going to create a super strong business. Okay. So people don't care about how the, how are the work out is if anything, sometimes I can dissuade them because they're like, I don't want to show up because it's so hard. The second thing is that results don't matter for retention. A lot of times it's just the experience and the connections that those people feel with your business. So I'm sorry. This is the main point. And so the question is, what are you doing to out compete everyone else on your customer experience and your customer service? If you're not actively investing in it and like holding workshops every week with your team about how to improve in Hawaii customers, you are being left behind. And, and just as a point of that, you're not going to compete on having a nicer facility because 24 hour fitness, nearly fitness will always have a nicer gym than you. All right. And they charge away less than you. So you don't want to get into that competing space because as soon as people associate, they're going to be like, well, they're way nicer and they're way cheaper. And it's going to be really hard to fight that fight. But this fight, those guys have shitty customer service. They have a terrible customer experience. That is where you can out compete them. And that is where you can win. And that is where you can get margin. That's where people are, we're for like crazy. And that's where you have loyal customers, not satisfied customers. Awesome.
